intro:
The Table of Nations in Genesis 10 is a genealogical survey distributing the descendants of Noah's three sons — Shem, Ham, and Japheth — among the peoples of the known ancient Near Eastern world, listing 70 (or 72 in the LXX) peoples. Japheth's descendants (10:2-5) correspond broadly to the peoples of the north and west — Anatolia, the Aegean, and Europe; Ham's descendants (10:6-20) to Egypt, Canaan, and Africa; Shem's descendants (10:21-31) to the Semitic peoples of the ancient Near East, culminating in the line leading to Abraham (Gen. 11:10-26). The list is not exhaustive in a modern ethnographic sense but represents the known world from a Levantine perspective, functioning as a theological ethnography of post-flood humanity.

The Table of Nations has several theological functions in the canonical narrative. First, it demonstrates that all humanity descended from Noah and thus from Adam — the common origin of all peoples underlies both the universality of judgment and the scope of promise. Second, it contextualizes the call of Abraham (Gen. 12:1-3) within the universal fragmentation of Babel (Gen. 11:1-9): God's election of one family to bless all families is the particular answer to a universal problem. Third, the seventy nations became a standard reference point in both testaments: Deuteronomy 32:8 assigns the nations to divine beings in proportion to the sons of Israel; Luke 10:1 sends seventy (or seventy-two) disciples to represent the harvest from all nations. Paul's Areopagus sermon cites the common origin of all nations as the basis for universal accountability to God (Acts 17:26).
